title: How to Make Compost Tea for Plants
excerpt: Compost tea is a simple liquid made by steeping finished compost in water. When made from good compost and used properly, it can give plants a gentle boost and support healthy soil as part of a broader natural gardening routine.
author: Permatable Team
content:
Liquid Compost

Compost tea is a simple liquid made by soaking finished compost in water. Gardeners use it to give plants a gentle nutrient boost and introduce beneficial microbes to the soil. When made properly, compost tea can be a useful addition to a natural gardening routine.

It is not a magic solution, and it does not replace healthy soil or regular compost use. But it can be a practical way to get even more value from the compost you already have.

What Is Compost Tea?

Compost tea is a water-based extract of finished compost. As the compost sits in water, some of its soluble nutrients and microorganisms move into the liquid. That liquid can then be used around plants.

There are two common approaches:

  • Simple compost tea made by steeping compost in water
  • Aerated compost tea made with added oxygen during brewing

For most home gardeners, a simple version is the easiest place to start.

Why Gardeners Use Compost Tea

Compost tea is popular because it is easy to make and easy to apply. It can be used on garden beds, vegetables, flowers, shrubs, and even container plants.

Gardeners often use compost tea to:

  • Give plants a mild nutrient boost
  • Support healthy soil biology
  • Water transplants more gently
  • Make use of finished compost in liquid form
  • Feed the soil around established plants

It works best as part of a broader soil-building approach, not as a replacement for compost, mulch, or good watering habits.

What You Need to Make Compost Tea

A basic compost tea setup is very simple.

You will need:

  • Finished compost
  • Water
  • A bucket, container, or large jar
  • A cloth, mesh bag, or old pillowcase if you want easier straining
  • A stick or spoon for stirring

If your tap water is heavily chlorinated, letting it sit out for a while before use may be helpful.

Start With Good Compost

The quality of your compost tea depends on the quality of the compost you use. Always use finished compost that smells earthy and looks dark and crumbly.

Good compost for tea should be:

  • Well decomposed
  • Free of foul odors
  • Made from safe ingredients
  • Not slimy or unfinished

Do not use compost that still contains a lot of fresh food scraps or smells rotten.

Simple Compost Tea Recipe

A common beginner method is very straightforward.

Basic Method

  1. Fill a bucket with water.
  2. Add finished compost, roughly 1 part compost to 5 parts water.
  3. Stir the mixture well.
  4. Let it steep for about 24 to 48 hours.
  5. Stir once or twice during that time.
  6. Strain if needed before using.

This creates a mild liquid that can be applied to the soil around plants.

The exact ratio does not need to be perfect. A weaker tea is usually safer than making it too strong.

How to Use a Compost Bag

Some gardeners place compost in a cloth bag before steeping it, almost like making a large tea bag.

This makes cleanup easier and helps reduce clogging if you plan to pour the liquid into a watering can.

You can use:

  • Cheesecloth
  • Burlap
  • An old cotton pillowcase
  • A mesh bag

After steeping, squeeze the bag lightly and return the solids to the compost pile or garden.

Can You Aerate Compost Tea?

Yes. Some gardeners make aerated compost tea by bubbling air through the water with an aquarium pump during brewing. This is done to increase oxygen and encourage aerobic microbial activity.

Aerated compost tea is more involved and requires:

  • A pump
  • Tubing
  • A brewing container
  • Closer attention to timing and cleanliness

For beginners, a simple steeped compost tea is usually the easiest and safest method.

How to Apply Compost Tea

Compost tea is most often used as a soil drench. That means pouring it around the base of plants so it reaches the root zone.

You can use it:

  • Around vegetables
  • On flower beds
  • For shrubs and small trees
  • In raised beds
  • On container plants
  • When transplanting seedlings

Apply it to moist soil when possible, rather than to extremely dry ground.

Some gardeners also spray compost tea on leaves, but for beginners, soil application is simpler and more reliable.

How Often to Use It

Compost tea does not need to be used every day or even every week. For most home gardens, occasional use is enough.

A common approach is to use it:

  • During active growing periods
  • After transplanting
  • Every few weeks in containers
  • When plants need gentle support

It is best treated as a supplement, not a constant feed.

When to Use Compost Tea

Compost tea can be especially useful:

  • In spring when plants begin active growth
  • After planting or transplanting
  • During mid-season for vegetables and flowers
  • For container plants that need regular care
  • After soil disturbance or stress

Using it as part of regular garden care can help support steady plant growth.

What Compost Tea Can and Cannot Do

Compost tea can be useful, but it helps to have realistic expectations.

What It Can Do

  • Provide a mild nutrient source
  • Help distribute some beneficial microbes
  • Support soil health
  • Make compost easier to apply in liquid form

What It Cannot Do

  • Instantly fix poor soil
  • Replace finished compost in the long term
  • Solve major plant disease problems on its own
  • Act like a powerful synthetic fertilizer

Healthy plants still depend mostly on good soil, correct watering, sunlight, and proper plant care.

Common Mistakes to Avoid

A few mistakes can make compost tea less effective or unpleasant to use.

  • Using unfinished compost: if the compost is not mature, the tea may smell bad or be less stable
  • Letting it sit too long: simple compost tea is best used soon after brewing
  • Making it too strong: a very concentrated mixture is not usually necessary
  • Using poor-quality compost: bad compost makes bad compost tea
  • Expecting it to replace everything else: compost tea works best as one tool among many in a healthy garden routine

How Long Does Compost Tea Last?

Fresh compost tea is best used soon after it is made, ideally within a day after brewing and straining. It is not something that stores well for long periods.

If left sitting too long, the microbial balance can change and the tea may lose quality. For that reason, make only what you plan to use fairly soon.

What to Do With the Leftover Solids

After straining the tea, the leftover compost solids do not need to be wasted.

You can:

  • Add them back to the compost pile
  • Spread them around garden beds
  • Place them around shrubs or trees
  • Mix them into soil

They still contain value, even after steeping.

Final Thoughts

Compost tea is an easy way to turn finished compost into a useful liquid for your garden. When made from good compost and used properly, it can give plants a gentle boost and help support healthy soil.

It is best seen as a supplement rather than a cure-all. The strongest gardens still rely on rich soil, regular compost, mulch, and good growing practices. But as a simple extra step, compost tea can be a helpful and rewarding addition to your routine.
